examples.title: Inverse of 2×2 Matrix
examples.description: Program to find inverse of a 2×2 matrix
examples.code:
def inverse_2x2(matrix):
    a, b = matrix[0]
    c, d = matrix[1]
    det = a*d - b*c
    if det == 0:
        return "Matrix is singular (no inverse)"
    return [[d/det, -b/det], [-c/det, a/det]]

# Example: [[2,1],[1,1]]
A = [[2, 1], [1, 1]]
print(inverse_2x2(A))  # Output: [[1, -1], [-1, 2]]
examples.explanation: Calculates determinant first, then applies 2×2 inverse formula: swap diagonals, negate off-diagonals, divide by determinant

examples.title: Gauss-Jordan Inverse Function
examples.description: C program to find inverse of n×n matrix using Gauss-Jordan
examples.explanation: Creates augmented matrix [A|I], performs row operations to convert left half to identity, right half becomes inverse

examples.title: Inverse Using NumPy
examples.description: Using Python's NumPy library for matrix inversion
examples.code:
import numpy as np

def matrix_inverse(matrix):
    A = np.array(matrix)
    det = np.linalg.det(A)
    if det == 0:
        return "Singular matrix - no inverse exists"
    return np.linalg.inv(A)

# Example: 3×3 matrix
A = [[2, 1, 1],
     [1, 2, 1],
     [1, 1, 2]]

inv = matrix_inverse(A)
print("Inverse:\n", inv)
print("Verification (A @ A⁻¹):\n", np.round(A @ inv))
examples.explanation: NumPy's linalg.inv() uses LU decomposition internally. Verify by A @ A⁻¹ ≈ I

examples.title: Determinant and Inverse using Cofactor Expansion
examples.description: Algorithm to find inverse using cofactor (adjugate) method
examples.code:
ALGORITHM FindInverseByCofactor(A)
BEGIN
  n ← ROWS(A)
  det ← Determinant(A, n)
  IF det = 0 THEN
    RETURN "Singular matrix - no inverse"
  ENDIF
  
  FOR i ← 1 TO n DO
    FOR j ← 1 TO n DO
      cofactor[i][j] ← (-1)^(i+j) × Determinant(Minor(A, i, j), n-1)
    ENDFOR
  ENDFOR
  
  adjugate ← Transpose(cofactor)
  inverse ← (1/det) × adjugate
  RETURN inverse
END

ALGORITHM Determinant(A, n)
BEGIN
  IF n = 1 THEN RETURN A[1][1]
  det ← 0
  FOR j ← 1 TO n DO
    det ← det + (-1)^(1+j) × A[1][j] × Determinant(Minor(A, 1, j), n-1)
  ENDFOR
  RETURN det
END
examples.explanation: Uses recursive cofactor expansion to find determinant, then builds cofactor matrix, transposes to get adjugate, divides by det for inverse
examples.timeComplexity: O(n!) - very inefficient for large n
